title: Quantum Domain Decomposition for Preconditioning the Finite Element Method
authors: Elise Fressart, Michel Nowak, Nicole Spillane
abstract: Even in cases where quantum linear solvers provide significant speedup compared to their classical counterparts, their performance depends on some of the same parameters. In particular, the condition number of the matrix which is to be inverted is a decisive parameter. A well known classical, and now quantum, remedy is to precondition the linear system $A x = b$ by premultiplying it by a matrix $H$ in such a way that the condition number of $HA$ is significantly smaller than the condition number of $A$.   In this work, we focus on a family of preconditioners called domain decomposition. First, we prove that it is feasible to apply quantum domain decomposition. We provide upper bounds for the block-encoding parameters of the Poisson problem discretized by the finite element method and preconditioned by the two-level Additive Schwarz preconditioner (one of the most fundamental domain decomposition techniques). From these bounds, we deduce the complexity of the quantum linear system solver. Second, we focus on a particular choice of local solver within the domain decomposition preconditioner by applying recent work by [Deiml and Peterseim, \textit{Math. Comput.}, 2025] on the Bramble--Pasciak--Xu (BPX) preconditioner. Finally, we provide details on how the operators are implemented.
summary: This paper introduces a quantum-compatible domain decomposition framework to precondition large-scale linear systems. By reducing the condition number of FEM-discretized PDEs, it enables more efficient execution of quantum linear solvers, potentially overcoming the scaling limits of current quantum algorithms.
main_problem: Addressing the scalability bottleneck in Quantum Linear System Solvers (QLSS) by reducing the condition number of large-scale matrices arising from the Finite Element Method (FEM).
main_result: The authors prove the feasibility of quantum domain decomposition and provide upper bounds for the block-encoding parameters and computational complexity of the preconditioned system.
method: The study utilizes a two-level Additive Schwarz preconditioner with BPX local solvers, implemented via block-encoding and Quantum Singular Value Transformation (QSVT) for the Poisson equation.
model_or_system: The mathematical model is the Poisson equation (a diffusion-type PDE) on a d-dimensional hyperrectangle, discretized using the Finite Element Method (FEM) on a Cartesian mesh.
key_observables: The condition number of the preconditioned operator, the subnormalization factor (alpha), and the complexity of computing inner products (quantities of interest).
important_parameters: Mesh size (h or L), overlap parameter (delta), number of subdomains (N), and the diffusion tensor (rho).
main_assumptions: The domain is assumed to be a hyperrectangle, the diffusion coefficient is constant over each mesh element, and the local solvers are implemented via block-encoding.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the spatial arrangement of overlapping subdomains in a 2D setup; numerical tables compare condition numbers and eigenvalue ratios across different preconditioners and subdomain counts.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from the FEM discretization setting to domain decomposition and spectral bounds, followed by block-encoding construction, cost analysis, and a numerical proof-of-concept.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Example of 8 overlapping subdomains (d = 2, N1 = 4, N2 = 2, L = 3, and δ = 2−L).
